patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH 0/8] per-memcg-per-node kmem accounting

series_items.subject: [PATCH 1/8] mm: memcontrol: propagate NMI slab stats to memcg vmstats
series_items.patch_summary: This patch series addresses an inconsistency in the propagation of NMI slab statistics to memcg-level vmstats. The current implementation, flush_nmi_stats(), only drains per-node NMI slab atomics into per-node lruvec_stats but fails to propagate them to memcg-level vmstats. This is inconsistent with account_slab_nmi_safe() which updates both per-node lruvec_stats and memcg-level vmstats. The patch series fixes this by propagating the NMI slab stats to memcg-level vmstats.

review_comments.author: Shakeel Butt
review_comments.summary: Provided feedback on the commit message and suggested rewording to clarify the issue. Also pointed out a nit about keeping code changes together. Acknowledged the patch after addressing these issues.

review_comments.author: Shakeel Butt
review_comments.summary: Requested the patch author to resend the patch as an independent patch and CC stable. This indicates that the reviewer is still considering the patch but wants it to be properly reviewed before applying.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Mon, May 11, 2026 at 03:49:36PM -0700, Shakeel Butt wrote:
> On Mon, May 11, 2026 at 10:20:36PM +0200, Alexandre Ghiti wrote:
> > flush_nmi_stats() drains per-node NMI slab atomics into the per-node
> > lruvec_stats, but does not propagate them to the memcg-level vmstats.
> > 
> > This is inconsistent with account_slab_nmi_safe() which updates both,
> 
[...]
> 
> > so fix this by propagating the NMI slab stats to the memcg-level vmstats.
> > 
> > Fixes: 940b01fc8dc1 ("memcg: nmi safe memcg stats for specific archs")
> > Signed-off-by: Alexandre Ghiti <alex@ghiti.fr>

Please resend this patch as independent patch and CC stable.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 2/8] mm: percpu: charge obj_exts allocation with __GFP_ACCOUNT
series_items.patch_summary: This patch modifies the per-cpu memory allocation system to charge the metadata and payload allocations separately, allowing for more accurate accounting of memory usage. The patch does this by passing the __GFP_ACCOUNT flag to the obj_exts allocation and removing its accounting in pcpu_memcg_pre_alloc_hook(). This is a preparatory step for upcoming per-memcg-per-node kmem accounting. The changes affect two files: mm/percpu-internal.h and mm/percpu.c, with 11 insertions and 20 deletions.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 3/8] mm: percpu: Split memcg charging and kmem accounting
series_items.patch_summary: This patch splits memory group (memcg) charging and kernel memory (kmem) accounting into two separate steps to improve per-memcg-per-node kmem accounting. The first step, obj_cgroup_precharge(), reserves pages for memcg limit enforcement without updating kmem stats. The second step, obj_cgroup_account_kmem(), accounts kmem after the allocation succeeds and places any remaining sub-page remainder into the object's stock. This decoupling allows for temporary overcharging of memcg but ensures that kmem accounting is accurate in the long run.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 4/8] mm: memcontrol: track MEMCG_KMEM per NUMA node
series_items.patch_summary: This patch removes the MEMCG_KMEM feature and instead introduces per-node obj_cgroup objects to track memory usage. It does not convert existing kmem users to proper per-memcg-per-node accounting, which is handled in subsequent patches. The changes include updates to memcontrol.h, mmzone.h, and memcontrol.c files.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 5/8] mm: memcontrol: per-node kmem accounting for page charges
series_items.patch_summary: This patch updates the __memcg_kmem_charge_page function to use per-node object groups for correct NUMA attribution of kernel memory. The change involves introducing a new variable nid_objcg, which is used to get the object group associated with the node where the page resides. This ensures that kernel memory charges are attributed correctly based on the node where the page is allocated.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 6/8] mm: slab: per-node kmem accounting for slab
series_items.patch_summary: This patch updates the slab hook to use per-node object cgroups for accurate NUMA attribution of kernel memory usage (NR_KMEM). It achieves this by introducing a new nid_objcg variable, which retrieves the per-node object cgroup based on the node ID of the slab. The patch modifies several functions within the memcontrol.c file to utilize the nid_objcg instead of the global objcg. This change ensures that kernel memory usage is attributed correctly to each NUMA node, providing a more accurate picture of system resource utilization.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 7/8] mm: percpu: per-node kmem accounting using local credit
series_items.patch_summary: This patch introduces per-node kmem accounting using a local credit system to handle precharged pages in the percpu memory allocation code. The previous approach used obj_stock, which is no longer suitable due to decoupling of memcg charging and kmem accounting. The new approach ensures that enough charged pages are available for allocation by maintaining a node-specific credit that gets refilled into the stock when necessary.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 8/8] mm: zswap: per-node kmem accounting for zswap/zsmalloc
series_items.patch_summary: This patch updates the zswap and zsmalloc modules to use per-node object cgroup for kernel memory accounting, ensuring that compressed page charges are attributed to the correct NUMA node. However, it does not correctly handle entries that span multiple pages, which will be addressed in a separate series by Joshua Hahn. The patch introduces a new function `zs_handle_to_nid` to determine the NUMA node associated with a given zsmalloc handle and updates the `zswap_store_page` function to use per-node object cgroup for charging. This change is necessary to accurately account for memory usage on systems with multiple NUMA nodes.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series introduces per-memcg-per-node kernel memory accounting to associate memcg and physical memory usage. It builds on recent changes to obj_cgroup, which ties object groups to their NUMA node. The patches split kmem charging and accounting for percpu allocations, and convert other kmem users such as slab and zswap to use the new per-node accounting. This allows for more accurate tracking of kernel memory usage at a per-node level.

review_comments.author: Shakeel Butt
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer Shakeel Butt noted that in non-NMI cases, account_slab_nmi_safe() updates both per-node lruvec_stats and memcg-level vmstats, but the patch is missing flushing of memcg-level vmstats, suggesting adding a call to flush_nmi_stats() for this purpose.

Reviewer Shakeel Butt noted that the commit message was fixed and nits addressed, allowing him to add an Acked-by tag.
review_comments.raw_body:
I think the above sentence needs clarification. Something like "For non nmi
case, account_slab_nmi_safe() calls mod_memcg_lruvec_state() which updates both
per-node lruvec_stats and memcg-level vmstats, so flush_nmi_stats() needs to
flush to per-node lruvec_stats as well as memcg-level vmstats but at the moment
the memcg-level vmstats flushing is missing. Fix that".

---

With the commit message fixed and nits addressed, you can add:

Acked-by: Shakeel Butt <shakeel.butt@linux.dev>

and thanks for catching this issue.

review_comments.author: Joshua Hahn
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer Joshua Hahn expressed concerns about the performance impact of the patch, specifically suggesting that expensive operations would benefit from benchmarking to demonstrate kernel allocation overhead.

Reviewer Joshua Hahn expressed concerns about the performance implications of splitting obj_cgroup to be per-node, which would invalidate the obj_stock cache and lead to increased atomic operations. He also questioned the need for iterating over num_possible_cpus() x pages_per_alloc.
review_comments.raw_body:
Hello Alex,

Thank you for your work!

Overall I think the direction makes sense to me. Pre-overcharging makes sense to
me as an approach, we would much rather overaccount than underaccount and
later have to breach limits.

I do have some concerns on performance, though. Namely, I think there are
some expensive operations that I think would benefit from some performane
benchmarking with this patch added (maybe some simple microbenchmarks that
demonstrates kernel allocation overhead could be useful).

---

to do with iterating over num_possible_cpus() x pages_per_alloc, which
doesn't seem trivial to me.

Another concern that I see is the stock credit system. Maybe we could be
bypassing the stock check leading to more time spent doing the atomic
operations.

obj_stock caches a single obj_cgroup, which means that if we split the objcg
to be per-node (in patch 6), then the obj_stock basically gets invalidated
every operation since we iterate over more objcgs (even though we are in
the same logical objcg). Maybe I'm missing something?

I haven't taken a deep look at the implementation details but just wanted to
raise some high level items that I noticed. Of course, all of these concerns
are just theoretical, if you can show that the performance delta is not
noticable then all of my concerns don't matter.

I also want to talk more about the local credit system but let's first see
what the numbers are first.

Thanks again, Alex. And I really like patch 2 because it is a solution to
a problem that I ran into in my percpu tracking series that I couldn't think
of before! Thank you for solving my problem too : -)

Have a great day!
Joshua

review_comments.author: Alexandre Ghiti (author)
review_comments.summary:
Author acknowledged that the swapoff path needs to drop the per-vswap spinlock before calling try_to_unmap(), agreed to restructure in v2

Author is responding to a concern about potential bypassing of atomic operations in the patch series, but does not provide further information or explanation.

Author acknowledged a performance regression in the objcg split, agreed to rebase on top of Shakeel's fix and re-run microbenchmark

Author acknowledged that the swapoff path needs to drop the per-vswap spinlock before calling try_to_unmap(), agreed to restructure in v2.
review_comments.raw_body:
Hi Joshua,

On 5/18/26 16:57, Joshua Hahn wrote:

---

I'm not following on this one, which atomic operations do you see that 
could be bypassed?

---

The objcg split comes from commit 01b9da291c49 ("mm: memcontrol: convert 
objcg to be per-memcg per-node type")and the problem you describe is 
exactly what Shakeel is trying to fix [1].

But I remember trying a microbenchmark and noticed a +5% regression (on 
top of the 67% then...), I'll rebase this series on top of Shakeel's and 
re-run.

[1] 
https://lore.kernel.org/linux-mm/20260520053123.2709959-1-shakeel.butt@linux.dev/T/#m127d4969b105c046a2a21e3c79c963771007583d

---

Great then, thanks :)

Alex

review_comments.author: Joshua Hahn
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer expressed overall positive sentiment, but noted that he is looking forward to seeing the actual numbers and no specific technical concerns were raised.

Reviewer Joshua Hahn expressed concern about potential performance issues in the patch's per-node kmem accounting implementation when dealing with nested stock systems, specifically obj_cgroup stock and local credit 'stock'. He suggested optimizing the stock refill path by precharging all pages, then iterating over nid_objcgs to perform atomic operations.

Reviewer expressed general approval and appreciation for the patch series without raising any specific technical concerns or objections.
sentiment_signals: looking forward to seeing actual numbers
review_comments.raw_body:
Hi Alex,

That sounds great with me : -) Looking forward to the numbers!

---

So in my initial scan of the patch 7 I had a concern that if we have a nested
stock system (obj_cgroup stock and local credit "stock"), then we could
incur more work if these are out of sync; do extra work in the stock refill
path in obj_cgroup_precharge, and then do extra work on top in the loop
within the pcpu_memcg_post_alloc_hook (obj_cgroup_account_kmem does the
charging atomically I think).

So what I mean is, I'm not sure what the "size" is typically for
pcpu_memcg_post_alloc_hook. But it might be a worthwhile optimization to
do precharge all the pages, then for each cpu iterate over the pages to
figure out how many pages are used per nid (doing just math, not actually
doing the atomic adds), and then outside both of these loops just iterate
over every nid_objcg once to perform the atomic operation.

Maybe this is needed or not (depending on how big "size" typically is
and whether we go from doing O(1000) atomic adds --> O(10) or some
big reduction, but I just wanted to toss it out there as something that
could potentially be expensive.

---

Sounds like a great idea! Thanks again Alex, have a great day! : -)
Joshua

patches_reviewed.subject: Re: [PATCH 0/8] per-memcg-per-node kmem accounting
patches_reviewed.patch_summary: This patch series aims to improve kmem accounting by making it per-memcg-per-node, which is a more accurate and efficient way of tracking memory usage. The current implementation has been shown to have a significant performance regression, and this series attempts to address that issue. The approach taken involves reworking the objcg split introduced in a previous commit to reduce overhead.

review_comments.author: Joshua Hahn
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer questioned which atomic operations could be bypassed, indicating a lack of understanding about the patch's intent and requesting clarification.

reviewer noted that the patch may incur extra work in certain scenarios where obj_cgroup stock and local credit 'stock' are out of sync, specifically in the stock refill path and pcpu_memcg_post_alloc_hook, and suggested a potential optimization to precharge all pages before performing atomic operations

Reviewer questioned the need for atomic operations to be bypassed in certain scenarios and asked for clarification on which specific operations could be optimized.
review_comments.raw_body:
Hi Alex,

That sounds great with me : -) Looking forward to the numbers!

---

So in my initial scan of the patch 7 I had a concern that if we have a nested
stock system (obj_cgroup stock and local credit "stock"), then we could
incur more work if these are out of sync; do extra work in the stock refill
path in obj_cgroup_precharge, and then do extra work on top in the loop
within the pcpu_memcg_post_alloc_hook (obj_cgroup_account_kmem does the
charging atomically I think).

So what I mean is, I'm not sure what the "size" is typically for
pcpu_memcg_post_alloc_hook. But it might be a worthwhile optimization to
do precharge all the pages, then for each cpu iterate over the pages to
figure out how many pages are used per nid (doing just math, not actually
doing the atomic adds), and then outside both of these loops just iterate
over every nid_objcg once to perform the atomic operation.

Maybe this is needed or not (depending on how big "size" typically is
and whether we go from doing O(1000) atomic adds --> O(10) or some
big reduction, but I just wanted to toss it out there as something that
could potentially be expensive.

---

Sounds like a great idea! Thanks again Alex, have a great day! : -)
Joshua

patches_reviewed.subject: Re: [PATCH v2 2/6] btrfs: add delayed ordered extent support
Re: [PATCH v2 2/6] btrfs: add delayed ordered extent support
patches_reviewed.patch_summary: This patch introduces a new concept of 'delayed ordered extent' in Btrfs, which is a range of file extents without submitted I/O or space allocation. The existing code for finishing one ordered extent can be reused on the children of this delayed OE, with only minor modifications. The idea is to split the concepts of ordered extent and its parent into two separate entities: one representing the range of file extents in the rb-tree, and the other representing the I/O operations. This change simplifies the code and reduces the need for special handling of parent-child relationships between OEs.

review_comments.author: Qu Wenruo
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer noted that classic OEs are not directly equivalent to bio submissions, citing large OE sizes and limited bio size as reasons for multiple smaller bios being submitted for a single OE.

Reviewer suggested migrating non-compressed writes into layer OEs or split OEs to handle truncation cases better, but noted that implementing this would require significant extra work to simplify the structure and remove unnecessary members from parent OEs.

Reviewer Qu Wenruo expressed agreement with Boris's proposed change to split ordered extent concepts into two types: one representing a range of file and another for I/O, suggesting that this would simplify the code and eliminate conditional logic.
review_comments.raw_body:
To be a little strict, the classic OE is not 1:1 with bio submitted.

The truth is, we can have super large OE, up to 128MIB. But our bio can 
only be as large as 1MiB for the worst cases.
Thus we will have a lot of smaller bios submitted for the same OE.

---

I have a similar idea for the long run, to migrate the non-compressed 
writes into the layer OEs (or split OEs), so that we can handle the 
truncation cases better, without allocating a larger than needed data 
extent.

However going all in on layered OEs will need quite some extra work in 
simplifying the structure, a lot of members in the parent OE is no 
longer required and should be simplified for layered OE solution.

---

If we're fine with the current layered OE solution, I'm totally fine 
going that path, migrating all writes to layered OE, and simplify the 
structure.

Thanks,
Qu

review_comments.author: Boris Burkov (author)
review_comments.summary:
Author acknowledged that delayed OEs lack submitted IO and proposed a conceptual split between range-based OEs and classic I/O-based OEs, agreeing to consider this change in future revisions.

Author acknowledged Qu Wenruo's idea for splitting concepts and agreed to review the patch series again with this new perspective.
review_comments.raw_body:
Yes, my bad, I was thinking about the "submitting" but not the bio
coverage.

---

Sounds good to me, I will review the rest of the series with this in
mind.

patches_reviewed.subject: Re: [PATCH v2 1/6] btrfs: add skeleton for delayed btrfs bio
patches_reviewed.patch_summary: This patch introduces a new concept of 'delayed ordered extent' in Btrfs, which is a range of file extents without submitted I/O or space allocation. The existing code for finishing one ordered extent can be reused on the children of this delayed OE, with only minor modifications. The idea is to split the concepts of ordered extent and its parent into two separate entities: one representing the range of file extents in the rb-tree, and the other representing the I/O operations. This change simplifies the code and reduces the need for special handling of parent-child relationships between OEs.

review_comments.author: Qu Wenruo
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer Qu Wenruo analyzed the callers of btrfs_get_extent() and found that they all have different kinds of protection against EXTENT_MAP_DELAYED, including checks for last byte, writeback or wait ordered extents, extra handling before calling btrfs_get_extent(), exact submit path checking, and only caring about holes/prealloc. The reviewer noted that since the delayed OE is only for writeback, the folio is always uptodate.

Reviewer Qu Wenruo expressed concern that using a false bbio as an async placeholder may not be necessary for some callers of btrfs_get_extent() to handle delayed extents, and suggested reusing the same submission code for both real and delayed bio submissions.

Reviewer questioned the purpose of using a false bbio as an async placeholder in btrfs_get_extent(), noting that it serves a specific objective beyond mere convenience, and requested clarification on its importance.
sentiment_signals: reviewer requested changes to ensure protection against EXTENT_MAP_DELAYED
concern about potential incompatibility with existing callers
request for clarification on purpose of using a false bbio
review_comments.raw_body:
I checked all btrfs_get_extent() callers, they have all different kinds 
of protection:

- Check against EXTENT_MAP_LAST_BYTE

- Have writeback or wait ordered extents for the range
   Including:
   * Direct IO

- Extra handling before calling btrfs_get_extent()
   E.g. btrfs_do_readpage(), which checks uptodate bit before
   calling btrfs_get_extent().
   In our case, since the delayed OE is only for writeback, the
   folio is always uptodate.

- Exactly on the submit path
   E.g. submit_one_sector()

- Callers only care about holes/prealloc
   E.g. find_first_non_hole() and all callsites inside file.c.
   And some other callsites like btrfs_find_new_delalloc_bytes().

---

Yes, re-use the same submission code is the core.

The idea is to make sure we're going through the same submission, so 
eventually we can get rid of the async-submission.

---

It's not only for convenience, but it is the objective.

Thanks,
Qu

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v2] mm/page_alloc: fix defrag_mode for non-reclaimable allocations

version_history.subject: [PATCH] mm/page_alloc: fix defrag_mode for non-reclaimable allocations
[PATCH v2] mm/page_alloc: fix defrag_mode for non-reclaimable allocations

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch fixes a problem in the Linux kernel's page allocation system where non-reclaimable allocations, such as those made with GFP_ATOMIC, fail when defrag_mode is enabled. The issue arises because these allocations cannot invoke direct reclaim or compaction, which are necessary for defrag_mode to work correctly. To fix this, the patch clears the ALLOC_NOFRAGMENT flag and retries the allocation if it requests kswapd reclaim but cannot do direct reclaim itself. This change allows non-reclaimable allocations to fall back to other migration types when defrag_mode is enabled, preventing a large number of SLUB allocation failures under network-heavy workloads.

patches_reviewed.subject: Re: [PATCH] mm/page_alloc: fix defrag_mode for non-reclaimable allocations

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH] sunrpc: use kref_get_unless_zero in auth_domain_lookup
patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch fixes a bug in the auth_domain_lookup function of the sunrpc module by using kref_get_unless_zero instead of plain kref_get to acquire a reference to an auth_domain entry. This prevents a window where an entry is still linked on the hash list with refcount == 0, which could lead to a dangling reference and object freeing while the caller still holds a pointer to it. The patch applies the same pattern used in the sibling function auth_domain_find to preserve existing semantics.

review_comments.author: Chuck Lever
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer Chuck Lever questioned whether auth_domain_lookup() can encounter a 0-refcount entry due to kref_put_lock()'s behavior, which acquires the spinlock before decrementing the refcount.

Reviewer Chuck Lever raised concerns that using kref_get_unless_zero() in auth_domain_lookup() might silently mask a refcount imbalance or use-after-free issue, and also noted that skipping the zero-refcount entry could lead to inserting a duplicate auth_domain into the hash list.
review_comments.raw_body:
[Severity: High]
Is this description of kref_put_lock() accurate?

Looking at kref_put_lock(), it uses refcount_dec_and_lock() which acquires
the spinlock before the 1->0 transition is completed. Since
auth_domain_lookup() holds the same auth_domain_lock, is it actually possible
for it to encounter a 0-refcount entry under normal conditions?

---

[Severity: High]
If a 0-refcount entry is encountered here, wouldn't it indicate a severe
underlying refcount imbalance or use-after-free rather than a normal race?

By using kref_get_unless_zero() and continuing the search, might this silently
mask the root cause of the WARN instead of resolving it?

Furthermore, if the loop skips the 0-refcount entry and falls through to the
end of the function, won't it insert a duplicate auth_domain with the same
name into the hash list?


-- 
Chuck Lever

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton (author)
review_comments.summary: Author acknowledged that the patch doesn't fix a real issue, but plans to drop it anyway.
review_comments.raw_body:
Sashiko is correct. This codepath can't see a 0 refcount here. The
patch won't break anything, but it's not fixing anything either. Let's
just drop this one.
-- 
Jeff Layton <jlayton@kernel.org>

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH 0/4] kdevops: fixes for new ansible and some nfsd default config changes

version_history.subject: [PATCH 0/4] kdevops: fixes for new ansible and some nfsd default config changes

series_items.subject: [PATCH 1/4] ansible: fix bare conditionals for ansible-core 2.20+ strict booleans
series_items.patch_summary: This patch fixes issues in Ansible playbooks caused by changes to boolean handling in ansible-core 2.20 and later. Specifically, it addresses problems where variables were being treated as strings instead of booleans, leading to fatal errors or incorrect behavior. The patch updates various playbook files to use the correct syntax for evaluating booleans and converts string variables to proper booleans using filters such as |bool and |default(true). This ensures that Ansible playbooks continue to function correctly with the updated core version.

review_comments.author: Daniel Gomez
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer Daniel Gomez pointed out that the issue is not a Kconfig YAML problem, but rather an old legacy Makefile extra vars propagation issue, and suggested promoting these variables to Kconfig and cleaning them up from Makefiles.

Reviewer Daniel Gomez noted that the patch is unnecessary because the variable will always receive a default value, making the 'is defined' condition redundant.

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton (author)
review_comments.summary: Author is seeking clarification from Daniel Gomez regarding whether their patch should be merged first or if Daniel's change should be done on top of the author's patch.

Mostly NFS workloads with fstests, pynfs, etc.

I'm not clear -- do you want me to wait on merging this patch until
your change goes in, or will you be doing that on top of this one?

series_items.subject: [PATCH 2/4] gitr: enable verbose logging in the test harness
series_items.patch_summary: This patch enables verbose logging in the test harness of the gitr suite, making it easier to identify what went wrong when tests fail.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 3/4] nfsd: use more reasonable defaults for local VM testing
series_items.patch_summary: This patch updates the default settings for NFS server (nfsd) configuration in Linux kernel, specifically for local VM testing. The changes include increasing the number of threads from 8 to 128 and reducing the lease time and grace period from 90 seconds to 30 seconds. Additionally, it enables version 4.0 support, sets mountd to manage group IDs, and configures dynamic threading by default on newer kernels.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 4/4] devconfig: add some extra packages to the kdev tools
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds additional packages to the kdev tools installation process, specifically for Red Hat configurations. The new packages include strace, lsof, git, wireshark-cli, rsync, and time, which are useful for development and debugging tasks. This change is part of a larger effort to enhance the devconfig toolset.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series addresses issues in the kdevops project, specifically related to ansible version 2.20 and nfsd default configuration changes. The patches fix a problem where strings were being evaluated as booleans in ansible, and make some optional changes to existing workflows for local VM testing and package installation. The changes include updates to various playbooks and tasks to accommodate the new ansible behavior and reasonable defaults for nfsd configurations.

review_comments.author: Daniel Gomez
review_comments.summary:
Daniel Gomez noted that the issue is not related to Kconfig's yaml format, but rather an old legacy Makefile variable propagation problem, specifically in scripts/gen-hosts.Makefile where GENHOSTS_EXTRA_ARGS is set to a boolean value. He suggested promoting these variables to Kconfig and cleaning them up from Makefiles.

Reviewer Daniel Gomez noted that the 'is defined' condition in the patch is unnecessary because the variable already receives a default value, regardless of whether it's propagated or not.

patches_submitted.subject: [RFC PATCH 00/40] mm: reliable 1GB page allocation

series_items.subject: [RFC PATCH 01/40] mm: page_alloc: replace pageblock_flags bitmap with struct pageblock_data
series_items.patch_summary: This patch replaces the packed bitmap of pageblock flags with a per-pageblock struct containing its own flags word, reducing cache line touches in the free path and simplifying accessor code. The memory overhead is 8 bytes per pageblock, but this is considered negligible. No functional changes are made.

series_items.subject: [RFC PATCH 02/40] mm: page_alloc: per-cpu pageblock buddy allocator
series_items.patch_summary: This patch introduces a per-cpu pageblock buddy allocator to improve scalability and reduce contention on large machines. The current zone->lock bottleneck is addressed by extending the per-CPU page cache (PCP) to operate on whole pageblocks, eliminating affinity violations and reducing lock hold times. The PCP refill process is divided into four phases: recovering owned fragments, claiming whole pageblocks, grabbing sub-pageblock chunks, and traditional __rmqueue() with migratetype fallback. This approach enables PCP-level merging, reduces fragmentation, and allows the allocator to degrade gracefully when necessary.

series_items.subject: [RFC PATCH 03/40] mm: page_alloc: split-path PCP free with local-trylock + remote-llist
series_items.patch_summary: This patch addresses a lock-inversion risk in the page allocator's PCP free path by introducing a per-CPU remote free list (llist) that allows remote CPUs to enqueue pages for free without taking the zone->lock. The local CPU then absorbs these queued pages back onto its PCP buddy lists at the next opportunity, eliminating the need for a trylock-fallback approach and reducing lock contention. This change also includes various other optimizations, such as memory hot-add lazy init and improved handling of page allocation on dead CPUs.

series_items.subject: [RFC PATCH 04/40] mm: mm_init: fix zone assignment for pages in unavailable ranges
series_items.patch_summary: This patch fixes a bug in the Linux kernel's memory initialization code where pages in unavailable ranges are assigned to the wrong zone. The problem occurs when an unavailable range spans multiple zones, causing later page_zone() calls to return incorrect results. To fix this, the patch looks up the correct zone for each PFN in the hole during initialization, which has no performance impact since it only runs once at boot time.

series_items.subject: [RFC PATCH 05/40] mm: page_alloc: remove watermark boost mechanism
series_items.patch_summary: This patch removes the watermark boost mechanism from the Linux kernel's page allocation system. The mechanism, which was introduced to react to fragmentation events at the pageblock granularity, is no longer necessary due to changes in how superpageblocks handle anti-fragmentation. The boost would often cause unnecessary wakeups of kswapd even when there were plenty of free pages. The patch removes various related functions and data structures, including the ZONE_BOOSTED_WATERMARK flag and the watermark_boost_factor sysctl.

series_items.subject: [RFC PATCH 06/40] mm: page_alloc: async evacuation of stolen movable pageblocks
series_items.patch_summary: This patch introduces a lightweight asynchronous evacuation mechanism to prevent memory fragmentation caused by movable pageblocks being stolen for unmovable or reclaimable allocations. When a movable pageblock is claimed, a work item is queued to migrate the remaining movable pages out, allowing future allocations to be concentrated in fewer pageblocks and reducing fragmentation.

series_items.subject: [RFC PATCH 07/40] mm: page_alloc: track actual page contents in pageblock flags
series_items.patch_summary: This patch extends the pageblock_data flags to track the actual types of pages allocated within a pageblock, independent of its intended migratetype. The new flags PB_has_unmovable, PB_has_reclaimable, and PB_has_movable are set at allocation time and cleared on full pageblock free or when migration confirms no pages of that type remain. This allows for superpageblock-level steering decisions to direct future allocations to already-tainted regions, keeping clean regions available for large contiguous allocations.

series_items.subject: [RFC PATCH 08/40] mm: page_alloc: superpageblock metadata for 1GB anti-fragmentation
series_items.patch_summary: This patch introduces a new data structure called superpageblock, which groups pageblocks at a coarser granularity of 1GB to enable anti-fragmentation tracking. The superpageblock maintains counts of pageblocks by migratetype and a list_head for future organization by fullness category. This is done to steer unmovable/reclaimable allocations into already-tainted superpageblocks, preserving clean superpageblocks for 1GB hugepage allocation. The patch adds new definitions and data structures in the Linux kernel's mmzone.h and page_alloc.c files, with negligible overhead of ~48 bytes per superpageblock.

series_items.subject: [RFC PATCH 09/40] mm: page_alloc: support superpageblock resize for memory hotplug
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds support for resizing superpageblocks when memory is hotplugged. It introduces a new function `resize_zone_superpageblocks` that allocates a new superpageblock array covering the full zone span, copies existing superpageblocks, and initializes new ones for the added range. This ensures that pages in newly hotplugged regions are steered by superpageblocks, preventing them from bypassing anti-fragmentation steering entirely. The patch also adds a boolean flag `spb_kvmalloced` to track whether superpageblocks were allocated using kvmalloc (hotplug).

series_items.subject: [RFC PATCH 10/40] mm: page_alloc: add superpageblock fullness lists for allocation steering
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new data structure to track the fullness of superpageblocks in memory, which are groups of pageblocks that can be allocated together as a single unit. The fullness is tracked by five buckets (FULL, 75%, 50%, 25%, ALMOST_EMPTY) and two categories (CLEAN vs TAINTED), allowing for efficient allocation steering without sorting. A separate list tracks completely free superpageblocks. The patch also adds a debugfs interface to display the superpageblock information.

series_items.subject: [RFC PATCH 11/40] mm: page_alloc: steer pageblock stealing to tainted superpageblocks
series_items.patch_summary: This patch modifies the Linux kernel's page allocation mechanism to prioritize stealing pages from tainted superpageblocks when allocating unmovable or reclaimable memory. The goal is to preserve clean superpageblocks for future hugepage allocations, which are typically used for large memory requests. To achieve this, the patch introduces a bounded scan of the free list to find an alternative page from a tainted superpageblock if the original candidate page belongs to a clean superpageblock. This optimization aims to reduce fragmentation and improve memory allocation efficiency.

series_items.subject: [RFC PATCH 12/40] mm: page_alloc: steer movable allocations to fullest clean superpageblocks
series_items.patch_summary: This patch steers movable allocations in the Linux kernel to use the fullest clean superpageblock, which helps preserve empty superpageblocks for potential huge page allocation. It introduces two new functions: `sb_preferred_for_movable` and `__rmqueue_from_sb`, which are used to find the fullest clean superpageblock and allocate pages from it. The patch also fixes an oversight in the bulk allocator's handling of ALLOC_NOFRAGMENT, ensuring that it doesn't taint clean superpageblocks. This change aims to improve memory allocation efficiency and reduce fragmentation.

series_items.subject: [RFC PATCH 13/40] mm: page_alloc: extract claim_whole_block from try_to_claim_block
series_items.patch_summary: This patch refactors the logic of claiming a whole pageblock in the Linux kernel's memory management system. It extracts this logic into a standalone function called `claim_whole_block`, which handles the transition from free to used, changes the migratetype of the pageblock, splits the block if necessary, and updates the free lists. This refactoring is done as part of a larger series of patches that aim to improve memory management in the kernel. The patch does not introduce any functional changes but prepares for future reuse of this logic in other parts of the codebase.

series_items.subject: [RFC PATCH 14/40] mm: page_alloc: add per-superpageblock free lists
series_items.patch_summary: This patch introduces per-superpageblock (SPB) free lists to improve allocation steering and memory-hotplug correctness. The SPB free lists allow the allocator to target specific superpageblocks without walking the entire zone, making it more efficient. Additionally, the patch addresses two latent bugs related to memory-hotplug: one where list heads are not properly initialized on hot-add, and another where embedded free lists are not updated during resize operations. The patch takes a critical section lock to prevent concurrent access by the allocator while updating the per-SPB free lists.

series_items.subject: [RFC PATCH 15/40] mm: page_alloc: add background superpageblock defragmentation worker
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a background worker that defragments superpageblocks when free space is low. The worker evacuates movable pages from tainted superpageblocks, improving memory utilization and reducing fragmentation. Defragmentation is triggered when a tainted superpageblock has less than two free pageblocks or three pageblocks worth of free pages. The worker uses an event-driven approach to target specific superpageblocks in need of defragmentation, rather than scanning the entire system. This patch also introduces per-superpageblock defrag work and irq work fields for memory-hotplug correctness.

series_items.subject: [RFC PATCH 16/40] mm: compaction: walk per-superpageblock free lists for migration targets
series_items.patch_summary: This patch updates the compaction mechanism in Linux to walk per-superpageblock (SPB) free lists when searching for migration targets. This change is necessary because free pages are stored on per-SPB free lists, not zone-level free lists, and standard compaction's free-page scanner needs to account for this. The patch also updates the pageblock movability bookkeeping to ensure it stays correct during compaction. Additionally, it fixes a check for zones whose start_pfn is not aligned to pageblock_order.

series_items.subject: [RFC PATCH 17/40] mm: page_alloc: superpageblock-aware contiguous and higher order allocation
series_items.patch_summary: This patch introduces a new allocation strategy that leverages superpage block (SPB) metadata to find contiguous ranges of free pages, making large and contiguous allocations more efficient. It adds tracking of contiguous allocations in SPB metadata and modifies the compaction algorithm to use this information for THP/mTHP and high-order unmovable/reclaimable allocations. The patch also includes a fallback mechanism to traditional compaction if the SPB approach fails.

series_items.subject: [RFC PATCH 18/40] mm: page_alloc: prevent atomic allocations from tainting clean SPBs
series_items.patch_summary: This patch prevents atomic allocations from tainting clean superpageblocks (SPBs) by introducing a staged relaxation of the allocation process. The patch extends the __rmqueue_smallest function to search for sub-pageblock-order free blocks in tainted SPBs, and introduces an intermediate flag ALLOC_NOFRAG_TAINTED_OK that allows retrying with tainted SPBs before dropping protection entirely. Additionally, it bypasses the pageblock compatibility threshold when trying to claim a pageblock from a tainted SPB. The patch also preserves the behavior for callers that pass __GFP_NORETRY, which is intended to handle failures by returning NULL and letting the caller's fallback path take over.

series_items.subject: [RFC PATCH 19/40] mm: page_alloc: aggressively pack non-movable allocs in tainted SPBs on large systems
series_items.patch_summary: This patch introduces a new threshold, SPB_AGGRESSIVE_THRESHOLD, to control when the Linux kernel aggressively packs non-movable allocations into tainted superpageblocks (SPBs) on large systems. On systems with more than 8 superpageblocks, it relaxes the minimum order floor for tainted SPBs, allowing non-movable allocations to consume free space at any granularity. This is done to prevent unnecessary tainting of clean SPBs and preserve MOVABLE capacity within tainted SPBs. The patch preserves the original behavior on small systems with fewer superpageblocks.

series_items.subject: [RFC PATCH 20/40] mm: page_alloc: prefer reclaim over tainting clean superpageblocks
series_items.patch_summary: This patch modifies the Linux kernel's memory allocation algorithm to prefer reclaiming pages over tainting clean superpageblocks when allocating memory. The change aims to prevent unnecessary tainting of superpageblocks, which can lead to fragmentation and decreased performance. To achieve this, the patch reorders the allocation fallback cascade to try reclaim and compaction before tainting clean superpageblocks. It also preserves the ALLOC_NOFRAGMENT flag through the slowpath and drops it as a last resort when OOM is reached. Testing shows that this change maintains unmovable pageblocks at zero throughout heavy workloads, where previously 2-3 additional SPBs would become tainted.

series_items.subject: [RFC PATCH 21/40] mm: page_alloc: adopt partial pageblocks from tainted superpageblocks
series_items.patch_summary: This patch modifies the page allocation mechanism in Linux to adopt partially free pageblocks from tainted superpageblocks during refilling of the per-cpu page pool (PCP). It introduces a new phase in the multi-phase approach used by rmqueue_bulk, which searches for partially-free pageblocks with sub-pageblock buddy entries matching the requested migratetype. The patch aims to reduce fragmentation spread to clean superpageblocks and concentrates non-movable allocations into already-tainted superpageblocks.

series_items.subject: [RFC PATCH 22/40] mm: page_alloc: add CONFIG_DEBUG_VM sanity checks for SPB counters
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds sanity checks to the Linux kernel's page allocation code to ensure that per-superpageblock counters are accurate. It introduces a new function `spb_debug_check` that verifies that the counters for free, unmovable, reclaimable, and movable pages do not exceed the total number of pageblocks. The function is called after each operation that modifies these counters. Additionally, three existing warnings in the kernel's code have been relaxed to prevent spurious warnings when migrating or isolating pages.

series_items.subject: [RFC PATCH 23/40] mm: page_alloc: targeted evacuation and dynamic reserves for tainted SPBs
series_items.patch_summary: This patch reduces the proliferation of tainted superpageblocks by introducing dynamic reserves and targeted evacuation strategies. The reserve size is scaled based on the superpageblock size, allowing for more efficient use of memory. Before dropping ALLOC_NOFRAGMENT, the patch attempts to evacuate movable pages from tainted superpageblocks to prevent tainting clean ones. This approach aims to reduce memory fragmentation and improve performance.

series_items.subject: [RFC PATCH 24/40] mm: page_alloc: prevent UNMOVABLE/RECLAIMABLE mixing in pageblocks
series_items.patch_summary: This patch prevents the mixing of UNMOVABLE and RECLAIMABLE pages in pageblocks by tightening two paths in the page allocator: try_to_claim_block() and __rmqueue_steal(). The patch introduces a helper function noncompatible_cross_type() to detect the UNMOVABLE-RECLAIMABLE pair, and modifies these functions to prevent cross-type relabeling or stealing. This change aims to reduce internal fragmentation caused by sticky RECLAIMABLE pages in pageblocks with both types of pages.

series_items.subject: [RFC PATCH 25/40] mm: trigger deferred SPB evac when atomic allocs would taint a clean SPB
series_items.patch_summary: This patch introduces a mechanism to trigger deferred superpageblock (SPB) evacuation when an atomic allocation would otherwise taint a clean SPB. The goal is to prevent the tainting of clean SPBs by allowing the next allocation to claim from the tainted pool instead. This is achieved by scheduling a deferred spb_evacuate_for_order() on the zone's pgdat workqueue whenever a non-movable allocation attempts to claim a pageblock from an empty or clean superpageblock as a fallback. The trigger is gated by single-flight, throttle, and tainted-pool precheck inside queue_spb_evacuate(), making it safe to fire from this hot path without storming the workqueue.

series_items.subject: [RFC PATCH 26/40] mm: page_alloc: refuse fragmenting fallback for callers with cheap fallback
series_items.patch_summary: This patch modifies the Linux kernel's page allocation mechanism to refuse fragmentation fallbacks for certain callers, specifically those that have a cheap fallback path. The change is made to prevent early-boot failures in QEMU due to a coarse bail-out gate in the slowpath retry of get_page_from_freelist. A finer-grained refusal is introduced in __rmqueue, where it checks if the allocation can be absorbed by a tainted pageblock or free pages. If so, it returns NULL and increments the SPB_HIGHORDER_REFUSED counter. The patch also adds a new flag ALLOC_HIGHORDER_OPTIONAL to indicate callers with cheap fallback paths.

series_items.subject: [RFC PATCH 27/40] mm: page_alloc: cross-migratetype buddy borrow within tainted SPBs
series_items.patch_summary: This patch introduces two new passes in the page allocation process to allow cross-migratetype buddy borrowing within tainted Special Purpose Buffers (SPBs). The first pass, Pass 2c, allows UNMOV and RECL allocations to borrow from each other's free lists. The second pass, Pass 2d, enables non-movable allocations to borrow from the MOVABLE free list of a tainted SPB. This is done to reduce structural mismatches that can lead to fragmentation and wasted space in the system. The patch restricts these new passes to tainted SPBs only, ensuring that clean SPBs are not polluted with cross-type mixing.

series_items.subject: [RFC PATCH 28/40] mm: page_alloc: drive slab shrink from SPB anti-fragmentation pressure
series_items.patch_summary: This patch introduces a new slab shrink mechanism to address anti-fragmentation pressure in the Linux kernel. The mechanism is triggered by various events, including demand and supply pressures, as well as proactive high-water marks. It uses a per-pgdat irq_work to bridge from allocator-lock context to a workqueue, which calls shrink_slab() with the zone's nid to free objects until either no progress is made or a batch of pageblock-sized objects has been freed. This approach aims to prevent sustained slab growth and fragmentation by releasing pressure on tainted SPBs, allowing for more efficient memory management.

series_items.subject: [RFC PATCH 29/40] mm: page_reporting: walk per-superpageblock free lists
series_items.patch_summary: This patch refactors the page reporting cycle to correctly walk per-superpageblock free lists, which were introduced by the superpage rework. The previous implementation walked an empty zone-level list, causing virtio-balloon free page reporting to become a no-op on systems with superpageblocks. The patch introduces a new function `page_reporting_cycle_list` that takes an explicit list_head and a shared budget pointer, allowing it to iterate over all superpageblocks in the zone for the requested order and memory type. The patch also holds the memory hotplug read lock around the SPB walk to prevent dangling references. A fallback path is retained for zones whose SPB array has not yet been allocated.

series_items.subject: [RFC PATCH 30/40] mm: show_mem: collect migratetype letters from per-superpageblock lists
series_items.patch_summary: This patch updates the show_mem function to correctly display migratetype letters for each order in a zone. After the superpage block (SPB) rework, free pages are stored on per-superpageblock lists instead of zone-level free_area lists. The patch iterates over each SPB in the zone and checks if any migratetypes have free pages at that order. If all migratetypes have been seen, it exits early. The patch also handles zones whose SPB array has not yet been allocated by falling back to the zone-level free_area. This change ensures that the migratetype letters are accurately displayed in the show_mem output.

series_items.subject: [RFC PATCH 31/40] mm: page_alloc: per-(zone, order, mt) PASS_1 hint cache
series_items.patch_summary: This patch introduces a hint cache to improve the performance of page allocations in the Linux kernel. The cache stores the most recently used superpage block (SPB) for each zone, order, and migratetype tuple, allowing the kernel to quickly retrieve the next SPB to allocate from. This reduces the number of iterations required during page allocation, resulting in a significant reduction in walk depth and an increase in hint hit rate. The patch also includes measurements showing a 99% hint hit rate and a median walk depth reduction from ~50 SPBs to ~5 SPBs on a test system with a steady workload.

series_items.subject: [RFC PATCH 32/40] mm: debug: prevent infinite recursion in dump_page() with CMA
series_items.patch_summary: This patch prevents infinite recursion in the dump_page() function when dealing with Contiguous Memory Allocation (CMA). It does this by adding a guard to check if the page frame number is valid before resolving its zone and classifying it. This ensures that the function can safely report on pages without a meaningful zone, preventing the kernel from running out of stack due to recursive calls.

series_items.subject: [RFC PATCH 33/40] PM: hibernate: walk per-superpageblock free lists in mark_free_pages
series_items.patch_summary: This patch series proposes changes to the hibernation code in the Linux kernel, specifically to improve its handling of free pages after the introduction of superpage blocks (SPBs). The current implementation walks the buddy allocator's free lists and marks each page as free, but this approach is inefficient with SPBs. The proposed change wraps the existing per-page walk in an SPB iteration loop, ensuring that free pages are correctly marked as such.

review_comments.author: Rafael J. Wysocki
review_comments.summary: Suggested breaking down the code into a separate function to simplify the logic and improve readability. Proposed an alternative implementation using if-else statements instead of a loop.

series_items.subject: [RFC PATCH 34/40] btrfs: allocate eb-attached btree pages as movable
series_items.patch_summary: This patch modifies the Btrfs file system to allocate extent buffer pages attached to btree_inode as movable, allowing them to be migrated and reducing fragmentation in the page-superblock allocator. The change targets a dominant source of fragmentation observed on large test systems, where ~28 GB of btree_inode pages were parked across many tainted superpageblocks, preventing 1 GiB hugepage allocation from those regions. By adding the __GFP_MOVABLE flag to one allocation site and passing it through to other functions, the patch enables these pages to be moved to movable pageblocks, where they can be drained by the background defragger without needing a fallback LRU-sample approach.

review_comments.author: Boris Burkov
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer Boris Burkov pointed out that the patch's assumption about extent buffers being eligible for release_folio() after refs == 1 is incorrect, as they should be immediately released when a block gets cowed, and instead suggested that the folios of interest are those with refs == 1 that will return 0 to release_folio(), which can already be reclaimed by memory pressure based reclaim.

Reviewer Boris Burkov suggested adding a Reviewed-by tag and left a small code nit, but did not raise any technical concerns about the patch's functionality.

Reviewer Boris Burkov suggested that instead of passing a __GFP_MOVABLE boolean flag, the code should transparently pass along a GFP flag to let callers handle movable pages correctly.
review_comments.raw_body:
Small correction to a generally correct story:

every extent_buffer we have used (it is newly cowed or we have read it
for some reason) will settle into a state where extent_buffer->refs == 1
and it is eligible for release_folio(). If a block gets cowed, then its
extent_buffer should be fully released/freed right away, with a tricky
EXTENT_BUFFER_STALE bit dance to prevent accidental reuse during this
process.

So I don't think cow or trees moving around is all that relevant here.

The folios you are after are the ones which are sitting around with
extent_buffer->refs == 1 and which will return 0 to release_folio(),
which is any valid, non-stale extent_buffer no btrfs operation is
currently using (typically referred to from a btrfs_path). These are
already available to be reclaimed by memory pressure based reclaim,
drop_caches, etc.

---

I left a small code nit inline, but whether you take that suggestion or
leave it, you can add:

Reviewed-by: Boris Burkov <boris@bur.io>

Thanks for the patch,
Boris

---

My gut says it is nicer to just transparently pass along a gfp flag
rather than a carefully documented movable boolean and let the callers
do the right thing. Just a nit, I don't care strongly about it.

series_items.subject: [RFC PATCH 35/40] mm: page_alloc: refuse best-effort high-order allocs servable at lower orders
series_items.patch_summary: This patch prevents best-effort high-order memory allocations from succeeding when a lower order allocation would be sufficient. It detects this pattern by checking if the allocation request has __GFP_NOWARN set and __GFP_NOFAIL not set, and then checks if the tainted pool can plausibly serve a smaller or same order allocation on the caller's retry. If so, it refuses the current attempt instead of escalating. This fix is intended to prevent high-order allocations from succeeding when they have an order-decrement fallback, which would otherwise lead to unnecessary escalation.

series_items.subject: [RFC PATCH 36/40] mm: page_alloc: set ALLOC_NOFRAGMENT on alloc_frozen_pages_nolock_noprof
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds the ALLOC_NOFRAGMENT flag to the alloc_frozen_pages_nolock_noprof function in the Linux kernel's page allocator. This flag steers the allocation towards tainted-SPB sub-pageblock space before fragmenting clean superpageblocks, which helps prevent atomic kmalloc_nolock callers from tainting hugepages unnecessarily. The patch addresses a performance issue where these callers would hit the fallback path on every PCP miss and convert movable pageblocks to UNMOVABLE. The flag is purely a steering hint and does not introduce any sleeping locks or depend on __GFP_DIRECT_RECLAIM, making it safe for use in various contexts such as NMI, hardirq, and PREEMPT_RT.

series_items.subject: [RFC PATCH 37/40] mm: page_alloc: move spb_get_category and spb_tainted_reserve to mmzone.h
series_items.patch_summary: This patch moves two helper functions, `spb_get_category` and `spb_tainted_reserve`, from `mm/page_alloc.c` to `include/linux/mmzone.h`. The move is done to keep a single source of truth for these functions, which will be needed in multiple places including `mm/compaction.c`. The functions remain unchanged semantically. They are used to classify superpageblocks as clean or tainted based on their contents and to calculate the minimum reserve required by a tainted superpageblock to maintain allocatability without tainting fresh clean superpageblocks.

series_items.subject: [RFC PATCH 38/40] mm: compaction: skip empty tainted superpageblocks as migration source
series_items.patch_summary: This patch optimizes the Linux kernel's page migration process by skipping empty tainted superpageblocks as a potential source of migratable pages. The patch introduces a fast-path check in the isolate_migratepages_block function to quickly return if a tainted superpageblock has no movable content, thus avoiding unnecessary per-page scanning work. This optimization preserves correctness for tainted superpageblocks with movable content and reduces overhead for those without.

series_items.subject: [RFC PATCH 39/40] mm: compaction: respect tainted SPB reserve in destination selection
series_items.patch_summary: This patch modifies the Linux kernel's compaction algorithm to respect tainted superpageblocks (SPBs) when selecting destination pages for migration. The goal is to prevent compaction from eroding the reserve headroom in tainted SPBs, which is necessary for satisfying non-movable allocations without tainting fresh clean SPBs. The change introduces a check that refuses tainted-SPB pageblocks as compaction destinations once the number of free pages falls below the reserved amount. This bias towards producing free pageblocks inside clean SPBs can help with hugepage allocation and, combined with other changes, enables kcompactd to handle clean-SPB consolidation alone.

series_items.subject: [RFC PATCH 40/40] mm: page_alloc: SPB tracepoint instrumentation [DO-NOT-MERGE]
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds instrumentation to the Linux kernel's page allocation mechanism using tracepoints. It introduces new tracepoints to track various events related to memory allocation, including anti-fragmentation diagnostics. The patch bundles all these tracepoint definitions and call sites into a single commit for easier review before upstream submission. The instrumentation is designed for diagnostic purposes only, providing detailed information about memory allocation decisions and behavior.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series aims to improve the allocation of large memory blocks by dividing memory into superpageblocks and steering unmovable, reclaimable, and high-atomic allocations into those already tainted by such allocations. The goal is to leave as many 1GB superpageblocks available for movable allocations, which can be easily defragmented for regular or huge pages. Various strategies are employed to achieve this, including preferential allocation from tainted blocks, fallback to small pages for certain kernel allocations, and migration of movable memory when a block falls below a free threshold. This approach enables more extensive use of 2MB THPs and mTHPs, as well as reliable allocation of 1GB pages. The series is built on top of Johannes's PCPBuddy series, which aims to reduce the need for CPUs to get pages from zone free lists by relying on page block ownership.

review_comments.author: Rik Riel (author)
review_comments.summary: The author addressed a concern that the zone ID passed to init_unavailable_range() may not match the actual zone spanning each PFN in memory holes between memblock regions, which can cause incorrect page flags and crashes. The author fixed this by looking up the correct zone for each PFN in the hole, which has no performance impact since it's init-only code running once at boot.

review_comments.author: Rik Riel (author)
review_comments.summary: The author addressed a concern about hotplugged memory not being covered by superpageblocks, acknowledged the issue, and explained how they plan to fix it in a new function called resize_zone_superpageblocks(). This function will be called from move_pfn_range_to_zone() after the zone span has been updated. It will allocate a new superpageblock array with kvmalloc_node(), copy existing superpageblocks, and initialize new superpageblocks for the added range.

review_comments.author: Rik Riel (author)
review_comments.summary: The author addressed a concern about memory fragmentation caused by stealing movable pageblocks for unmovable or reclaimable allocations, and responded by adding an asynchronous evacuation mechanism to migrate remaining movable pages out of the claimed block, allowing future unmovable/reclaimable allocations to be concentrated in fewer pageblocks.

review_comments.author: Rik Riel (author)
review_comments.summary: The author addressed a concern about accurately tracking page contents in superpageblocks by extending the pageblock_data flags to include PB_has_unmovable, PB_has_reclaimable, and PB_has_movable bits. The new flags are set at allocation time and cleared on full pageblock free or when migration confirms no pages of that type remain. This change provides a foundation for superpageblock-level steering decisions.

review_comments.author: Rik Riel (author)
review_comments.summary: The author addressed a concern about lock inversion in the PCP free path, acknowledged that using spin_trylock and falling back to free_one_page() defeats the per-CPU pageset benefits, and proposed a solution involving a per-CPU remote free list (llist) consumed by the owning CPU. The author agreed to implement this change.

review_comments.author: Rik Riel (author)
review_comments.summary: The author addressed a concern about the watermark_boost mechanism, which was causing excessive wakeups of kswapd due to sub-pageblock fallbacks and order-0 reclaim. The author agreed that this mechanism is not effective for superpageblocks and decided to drop it entirely, removing various related code paths.

review_comments.author: Rik Riel (author)
review_comments.summary: The author addressed a concern about the overhead of tracking superpageblocks, explaining that the array is allocated per-zone during boot via memblock and has negligible overhead (~48 bytes per superpageblock). The author confirmed that this is pure bookkeeping with no allocation behavior change.

review_comments.author: Rik Riel (author)
review_comments.summary: Author addressed a concern about memory overhead and storage efficiency in the pageblock flags bitmap, agreed to replace it with a per-pageblock struct containing its own flags word, which eliminates bit-packing indexing and simplifies accessor code; no functional change is expected.

review_comments.author: Rik Riel (author)
review_comments.summary: Author addressed a concern about locking contention on large machines, explained that the proposed solution extends the page cluster (PCP) to operate on whole pageblocks with ownership tracking, and confirmed that this change will eliminate affinity violations and shorten zone->lock hold time. The author also noted that the allocator degrades gracefully to traditional behavior when PCP batch sizes are small or the zone is fragmented.

review_comments.author: Boris Burkov
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer Boris Burkov pointed out that the patch's focus on cow and tree movement is misplaced, as extent_buffers with refs == 1 are already eligible for release_folio() and can be reclaimed by memory pressure based reclaim or drop_caches. He suggested that the actual issue is with valid, non-stale extent_buffers not currently in use by btrfs operations, which should be available for reclaim.

Reviewer Boris Burkov suggested a minor code improvement and provided a Reviewed-by tag, indicating that he has reviewed the patch.

Reviewer Boris Burkov suggested simplifying the patch by using GFP flags instead of a movable boolean, stating that this would allow callers to handle allocations correctly without additional documentation.
review_comments.raw_body:
Small correction to a generally correct story:

every extent_buffer we have used (it is newly cowed or we have read it
for some reason) will settle into a state where extent_buffer->refs == 1
and it is eligible for release_folio(). If a block gets cowed, then its
extent_buffer should be fully released/freed right away, with a tricky
EXTENT_BUFFER_STALE bit dance to prevent accidental reuse during this
process.

So I don't think cow or trees moving around is all that relevant here.

The folios you are after are the ones which are sitting around with
extent_buffer->refs == 1 and which will return 0 to release_folio(),
which is any valid, non-stale extent_buffer no btrfs operation is
currently using (typically referred to from a btrfs_path). These are
already available to be reclaimed by memory pressure based reclaim,
drop_caches, etc.

---

I left a small code nit inline, but whether you take that suggestion or
leave it, you can add:

Reviewed-by: Boris Burkov <boris@bur.io>

Thanks for the patch,
Boris

---

My gut says it is nicer to just transparently pass along a gfp flag
rather than a carefully documented movable boolean and let the callers
do the right thing. Just a nit, I don't care strongly about it.

review_comments.author: Rafael Wysocki
review_comments.summary:
Rafael Wysocki suggested breaking out the code that updates the page_count value into a separate function, named do_mark_free_pages(), which would take list, buddy_list, and page_count as arguments.

Rafael Wysocki suggested a simplified version of the do_mark_free_pages() function call, replacing the existing logic with an if-else statement that checks if the zone has any superpageblocks before iterating over them.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v2] perf/ftrace: Fix WARNING in __unregister_ftrace_function
patches_submitted.patch_summary: The patch fixes a WARN_ON condition in __unregister_ftrace_function() by adding a check to ensure the ftrace_ops is enabled before unregistering it.

review_comments.author: Masami Hiramatsu (Google)
review_comments.summary: Approved the patch as it fixes a warning condition and added an Acked-by tag.

review_comments.author: Steven Rostedt
review_comments.summary: Asked Rik to reply to Sashiko's comment, but did not provide any substantive feedback on the patch.
review_comments.raw_body:
Rik,

Can you reply to Sashiko here?

The below comes from Sashiko: https://sashiko.dev/?list=org.kernel.vger.linux-trace-kernel#/patchset/20260513161916.04151502%40fangorn

Thanks,

-- Steve


> commit f90ac36f39b79a19c015522bb1caf7ef903caf02
> Author: Rik van Riel <riel@surriel.com>
> perf/ftrace: Fix WARNING in __unregister_ftrace_function
> This commit attempts to fix a WARN_ON triggered during perf_event_alloc() error
> cleanup by checking FTRACE_OPS_FL_ENABLED before unregistering the ftrace
> function. The warning occurs because the ftrace_ops registration failed or was
> torn down by perf_try_init_event() earlier, but the event->destroy callback is
> executed again during the error path.
> > diff --git a/kernel/trace/trace_event_perf.c b/kernel/trace/trace_event_perf.c
> > index 61e3a2620fa3c..8d64f17135e8f 100644
> > --- a/kernel/trace/trace_event_perf.c
> > +++ b/kernel/trace/trace_event_perf.c
> > @@ -495,7 +495,11 @@ static int perf_ftrace_function_register(struct perf_event *event)
> >  static int perf_ftrace_function_unregister(struct perf_event *event)
> >  {
> >  	struct ftrace_ops *ops = &event->ftrace_ops;
> > -	int ret = unregister_ftrace_function(ops);
> > +	int ret = 0;
> > +
> > +	if (ops->flags & FTRACE_OPS_FL_ENABLED)
> > +		ret = unregister_ftrace_function(ops);
> > +
> >  	ftrace_free_filter(ops);

> Does calling ftrace_free_filter() unconditionally here mask a double-teardown
> regression while leaving the underlying double-free active?
> In perf_try_init_event(), if a PMU event_init() succeeds but a subsequent
> capability check fails, it explicitly calls event->destroy(event) to roll back:
> kernel/events/core.c:perf_try_init_event() {
>     ...
> 		if (ret && event->destroy)
> 			event->destroy(event);
>     ...
> }
> However, it does not set event->destroy to NULL.
> When the event is later freed in error paths, the stale event->destroy callback
> executes a second time.
> If ftrace_free_filter() is called twice, won't it result in a direct
> double-free of ops->func_hash->filter_hash, since ftrace_free_filter() frees
> the hash but does not nullify the pointers?
> Additionally, for regular tracepoint events, wouldn't the second teardown
> execute perf_trace_event_unreg() again?
> This could double-decrement total_ref_count, potentially freeing the shared
> perf_trace_buf while other events are active, and trigger an unbalanced
> module_put() leading to module refcount underflows.
> Should the root cause be addressed by setting event->destroy = NULL in
> perf_try_init_event() immediately after invoking it?

> >  	return ret;
> >  }
